devi-kavacham: Before the great battle hymns of the Devi Mahatmyam (Markandeya Purana) are recited, practitioners traditionally put on this armour first. देवी कवचम् names the Goddess in twenty-eight cosmic forms, asking each to guard a different part of the body and the directions around the seeker. The whole text runs to 28 verses, about 12 minutes of recitation. Its purpose is plain in its title: a kavacha, a shield. Devotees turn to it to be freed of fear and steadied with courage and inner strength, while it also calls down wisdom, peace, prosperity, and spiritual light. Tuesdays and Fridays suit it well, and during Navratri it is rarely skipped, since it opens the larger Devi Mahatmyam recitation that follows.
devi-khadgamala-stotram: In the Sri Vidya tradition that traces to the Vamakeshvara Tantra, reciting देवी खड्गमाला स्तोत्रम् is held to equal a full Sri Chakra puja done by chant alone. The 'sword-garland' names, in order, every deity seated in the nine avaranas, the concentric enclosures of the Sri Chakra. Across its 9 sections, roughly 18 minutes of recitation, it summons the eight siddhis along with the ten further powers known together as the dashamahasiddhi. Adepts regard it as the most potent stotra in Sri Vidya for swift inner progress, and the surest way to clear obstacles and see desires fulfilled. It asks, finally, for ananta shri, boundless prosperity, and for moksha. Friday and Tuesday are the customary days; Navratri and Diwali, the customary seasons.
devi-mahatmyam-prathama-charitra: This is where the Durga Saptashati begins. The Prathama Charitra — देवी माहात्म्यम् प्रथम चरित्रम् — opens the Markandeya Purana (Devi Mahatmyam / Durga Saptashati) by telling the first manifestation of the Goddess as Mahakali, roused when Brahma sings to Yoganidra and begs the sleeping Vishnu to wake and confront Madhu and Kaitabha. Read across its 30 verses, a span of about 20 minutes, it is the opening movement of the Navratri recitation. The episode is understood to dissolve tamas, the inertia of ignorance, and to lift the veil of mahamaya so that higher awareness can stir. Those facing strong adversaries recite it for protection. Tuesdays and Fridays are favoured, and during Navratri it is read as a matter of course.
devi-mahatmyam-uttama-charitra: The Uttama Charitra carries the Durga Saptashati to its fiercest climax: the slaying of Shumbha and Nishumbha, and the moment Kali drinks every drop of Raktabija's blood before it can spawn new demons. Drawn from the Markandeya Purana (Devi Mahatmyam / Durga Saptashati), देवी माहात्म्यम् उत्तम चरित्रम् runs to 27 verses, about 30 minutes, and holds the beloved 'Ya Devi Sarva-bhuteshu' refrain, which finds the Goddess present in all beings as power, mercy, and peace. Here Kali and Chamunda are called to tear out negativity by its deepest root. The Devi's own pledge — to return whenever evil rises — gives the reciter lasting reassurance. The chapter is said to burn away even sattva-bound ego and open the door to liberation. Recite it Tuesdays and Fridays, and through Navratri, for victory and spiritual strength.
devi-suktam: Among the oldest hymns to the Divine Feminine, this one comes straight from the Rigveda (10.125), spoken in the first person by the Goddess herself — the rishika Vak declaring that she is the power moving through every god, every sage, every act of creation. देवी सूक्तम् is short, just 8 verses, about 5 minutes, yet it holds the primordial revelation of Shakti. To recite it is to ask for knowledge of that supreme cosmic power, for sovereignty and wisdom and inner illumination, and for the strength, valour, and protection the Goddess grants those who know her as all-pervading. Tuesdays and Fridays are traditional, and it has a natural place in Navratri worship.
dhanteras-katha: On Kartik Krishna Trayodashi, the story goes, Bhagavan Dhanvantari rose from the churning ocean bearing the pot of amrita — and the festival of Dhanteras marks that emergence. धनतेरस कथा gathers this Samudra Manthan account from Puranic tradition alongside the tale of Raja Hima's young son, whose devoted wife kept Yama at the threshold by lighting rows of diyas and heaping gold and silver at the door. Read across 20 verses in about 12 minutes, the katha explains why lamps are lit and precious metal bought on this day to ward off untimely death. It invokes Dhanvantari for health, long life, and freedom from disease, and the worship of Lakshmi and Kubera for wealth, closing with mantras to both. Essential listening for Dhanteras and the wider Diwali observance.
dhanteras-puja-stotram: Recited as the lamps are lit on Dhanteras evening, श्री धनतेरस पूजा स्तोत्रम् ties together the three blessings the day is meant to draw: health from Dhanvantari, the divine healer; wealth from Dhana-Lakshmi; and the security of the home through Kubera's grace. The hymn runs 10 verses, about 6 minutes. Central to it is the yama-dipa-dana, the lamp offered to Yama to turn aside the fear of untimely death — the rite that gives Dhanteras much of its meaning. It calls in gold, silver, and material plenty, and asks for sound health and freedom from disease. Devotees keep it for the Dhanteras and Diwali pujas, when its words match the auspiciousness of the occasion.
dhanvantari-chalisa: Forty chaupais frame this hymn to the physician-god, with opening and closing dohas rounding the recitation to its full 42 verse-units, about 15 minutes. धन्वन्तरि चालीसा addresses Dhanvantari as the deity of Ayurveda, the avatar of Vishnu who carried healing into the world. Patients and practitioners alike turn to it for recovery from illness, for longevity, and for a life unburdened by disease; recited with faith, it is said to settle the tridosha — vata, pitta, and kapha — into balance. Tradition also reaches for it against epidemics and long chronic ailments, and prays through it for the skill and success of those who practise medicine. Its power is felt most keenly on Dhanteras, observed as Dhanvantari Jayanti, though Tuesdays and Thursdays suit daily recitation too.
dhanvantari-dwadash-nama: Twelve names, one short prayer. The Dwadash Nama Stotram from the Bhagavata Purana sets out twelve epithets of Dhanvantari, the divine physician and the healing avatar of Vishnu who rose from the ocean of milk. धन्वन्तरि द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् is compact — 5 verses, near 3 minutes — which is part of its appeal for daily use. Recited each day, tradition associates it with relief from disease and bodily ailment, and with the blessing of a long, disease-free life. Patients keep it close; so do healthcare workers and students of Ayurveda. Tuesdays and Sundays are the recommended days, with Dhanteras and Dhanvantari Jayanti as the high points of the year.
dhanvantari-hridayam: A hridayam stotra claims to hold the very heart of its deity, and धन्वन्तरि हृदयम् does exactly that for healing. Drawn from the Agni Purana and the wider Ayurvedic tradition, it works through the body methodically — calling protection and repair to each system and organ in turn, naming the tridosha, the saptadhatu, and specific diseases as it goes. In 8 verses, about 5 minutes, it asks for freedom from every ailment and the steadiness of perfect health. The structure makes it a favourite of those who are unwell, of physicians, and of devotees drawn to Ayurveda. Tuesday is the traditional day, with Dhanteras and Dhanvantari Jayanti the occasions when it is recited with particular care.
dhanvantari-sahasranama: The thousand names of Dhanvantari, the divine physician, are condensed here into a recitation of 9 verses that takes about 6 minutes. Rooted in the Agni Purana and Ayurvedic tradition, धन्वन्तरि सहस्रनाम स्तोत्रम् is distinctive for the vocabulary it draws on — the names invoke the healer through genuine Ayurvedic terms, with explicit mention of the tridosha, the saptadhatu, and the foundational concepts of the science. Devotees recite it to seek freedom from all disease and a lengthening of life. It suits archana and puja as much as private prayer, and is a natural choice for Dhanteras observance and for the health prayers of Ayurveda practitioners. Tuesday is the day most often kept, alongside Dhanteras and Dhanvantari Jayanti.
dhanvantari-stotram: Dhanvantari is the divine physician and the deity of Ayurveda — an avatar of Vishnu who emerged from the Samudra Manthan with the vessel of immortal nectar. श्री धन्वन्तरि स्तोत्रम् is the classical hymn addressed to him, 8 verses long, about 6 minutes to recite. Its old reputation is for clearing away disease and easing two fears that shadow every life: the dread of old age and of death before its time. In their place it asks for long life, sound health, and freedom from every ailment. Doctors and Ayurveda practitioners hold it close to their work. Thursday is the customary day, and on Dhanteras, kept as Dhanvantari Jayanti, the stotra is recited for the year's blessing of health.
dharma-stuti: Here Dharma is not abstraction but presence — addressed as a living force, and bound up with Yama, the lord who upholds cosmic law. श्री धर्म स्तुति, gathered from the Mahabharata and the Manu Smriti, asks that righteousness itself stand guard over the one who recites it. Across 10 verses, roughly 7 minutes, it works to cultivate the ten classical qualities of dharma in the aspirant and to firm up the resolve to live rightly. It promises victory won through truth and justice rather than force. Tradition holds that it washes away accumulated sin and settles the devotee in virtue, opening, over time, the path to liberation. Saturdays and Thursdays suit it, and it has a fitting place in Gita Jayanti observance.
diwali-vrat-katha: No Diwali Lakshmi puja is complete without the katha that explains it. दिवाली व्रत कथा, drawn from the Skanda Purana and traditional sources, carries the full vrat narrative — the puja vidhi and mantras, the princess who kept death from her husband's door with rows of lit diyas and a heap of gold, and the homecoming of Lord Rama to Ayodhya that the festival's lamps recall. Read across 20 verses in about 12 minutes, it shows why light on this night drives back darkness and draws in divine favour. The story is told to invoke Lakshmi's blessing of wealth and lasting prosperity, to guard against untimely death, and to sweep away misfortune. It belongs to both Diwali and Dhanteras observance, with Ganesha and Rama honoured alongside the Goddess.
dnyaneshwari-pasaydan: Sant Dnyaneshwar ends his great Marathi commentary on the Gita not by asking for himself, but for the whole world. ज्ञानेश्वरी पसायदान, the closing prayer of Chapter 18 of the Dnyaneshwari, is among the most generous verses in all of Indian devotion — a plea for the welfare of every being without exception, that even wrongdoers be turned toward goodness and the darkness of sin lifted from the earth entire. Across 9 verses, about 5 minutes, it asks for the company of saintly people, the fulfilment of every righteous wish, and both worldly success and final liberation, all offered to Vitthal and to Krishna. Recite it on Thursdays and Mondays, and during Ashadhi Ekadashi, Kartiki Ekadashi, and Guru Purnima.
durga-ashtakam: Eight verses, drawn from the Devi Mahatmyam (Markandeya Purana), praise Durga as the single power behind creation, sustenance, and dissolution — the cosmos held in one Goddess. दुर्गा अष्टकम् takes about 5 minutes to recite. Its work is to cut through mahamoha, the great delusion that clouds the mind, and to replace it with wisdom; in its train come peace, patience, prosperity, and a settled contentment. Devotees turn to it to have desires granted by her grace, and to be shielded through kalaratri, the dark night, with every fear stripped away. The Tuesday and Friday days are traditional, and the verses gain force during Navratri and Dussehra.
durga-chalisa: Forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, with framing dohas bringing दुर्गा चालीसा to its full count of 40 verses, around 15 minutes of recitation. The hymn moves through all nine forms of Durga, the Navadurga, calling on each in turn. Devotees keep it for protection against enemies and evil forces, for courage in place of fear, and for recovery from illness and the gift of good health. Recited through Navratri, it is held to bring wishes to fruition, and beyond the worldly it promises both earthly joys and final liberation — bhukti and mukti together. Shiva is honoured alongside the Goddess. Tuesdays and Fridays are the days most often chosen.
durga-dvatrinsha-naamamala: Thirty-two names of Durga, strung together as a single garland of protection — that is what दुर्गा द्वात्रिंशन्नाममाला offers, drawn from the Devi Mahatmyam (Markandeya Purana). The text is brief, 8 verses and about 4 minutes, yet its reputation is large: the tradition holds that reciting it frees one from every fear, without doubt. Each name carries its own force, and together they are invoked for victory in battles, contests, and conflicts of every kind, for release from disease and bondage, and above all for the clearing of durgati — the misfortunes and obstacles the Goddess's very name promises to dispel. Its shortness makes it ideal for daily recitation. Tuesdays and Fridays are favoured, with Navratri and Dussehra the high seasons.
durga-mangalashtakam: Built in the mangala form, every verse here opens a benediction — and across its eight, दुर्गा मङ्गलाष्टकम् passes through all nine forms of the Navadurga in turn. Born of the Durga Saptashati tradition, the hymn runs about 5 minutes. It asks for release from calamity and danger, for the scattering of enemies, and for the obstacles strewn across one's path to be swept clear. In their place it sets courage, protection, and the steadying presence of the Divine Mother, with Parvati and Kali invoked alongside Durga. Fridays and Tuesdays carry it well, and it finds its fullest voice during Navratri, Durga Puja, and Vijayadashami.
durga-nav-shloki: For those who cannot sit with the full Saptashati, the Nav Shloki distils its power into nine essential verses. Drawn from the Markandeya Purana / Devi Mahatmya, दुर्गा नवश्लोकी is a compact but complete invocation of Durga's strength — 9 shlokas, about 4 minutes, and the most popular of the condensed Durga prayers for daily use. Its blessings are practical ones: relief from fear, poverty, sorrow, and disease; the scattering of enemies; the gift of shubha-buddhi, an intellect inclined toward the auspicious. Recited steadily, it is said to quiet every form of suffering. Tuesdays and Fridays are traditional, and it takes its natural place in Navratri and Durga Puja worship.
durga-pancharatnam: Five jewels — that is what pancharatnam means, and दुर्गा पञ्चरत्नम् sets out five verses, each a gem offered to the Goddess. From the Markandeya Purana, the hymn is short, about 3 minutes for its 5 verses, yet it gathers wide power: it calls on the Navadurga and the Dasha Mahavidya forms together, with Parvati and Kali in attendance. Devotees recite it to be relieved of disease, obstacle, and suffering, and to receive prosperity, health, and spiritual fulfilment as one undivided blessing. Its strength is felt most during Navratri and Durga Puja recitation. The Tuesday and Friday days are favoured, with Dussehra adding a further occasion.
durga-saptashloki: Seven shlokas hold the whole of the Durga Saptashati — that is the claim, and the gift, of दुर्गा सप्तश्लोकी. Drawn from the Durga Saptashati (Markandeya Purana), it is meant for those who cannot recite the full seven hundred verses but want their essence and their protection. The text runs 7 verses, about 3 minutes, and can be chanted quickly whenever shelter is needed at once. It works to lift poverty, sorrow, fear, and every affliction, to clear enemies and obstacles from the devotee's road, and to draw in auspiciousness while fulfilling righteous desires. Vishnu, Lakshmi, Saraswati, and Kali are all invoked through it. Recite it Tuesdays and Fridays, and through Navratri.
durga-suktam: This Vedic hymn names Durga as the one who carries the seeker across — the boat that bears a soul over the ocean of difficulty. Drawn from the Taittiriya Aranyaka (4.10) of the Mahanarayana Upanishad, दुर्गा सूक्तम् is 7 verses, about 7 minutes, and includes the Durga Gayatri for a complete invocation. Its imagery is of crossing: obstacles and dangers removed from the path, enemies and hostile forces turned back, and courage given to face the hardest of situations. Tradition links it too with auspiciousness and good fortune flowing into the household. Tuesdays and Fridays are the recommended days, and during Navratri it is recited for the Goddess who ferries her devotees to safety.
dussehra-stotram: Vijayadashami marks the day Rama felled Ravana, and दशहरा स्तोत्रम् is the prayer that celebrates that victory. Drawn from the Valmiki Ramayana and the broader Vijayadashami tradition, with Hanuman and Sita honoured beside Rama, the hymn runs 10 verses, about 7 minutes. Recited on Dussehra, it is held to burn away the ten kinds of sin — the dasha-papa the festival is named to dispel — and to grant victory in every direction by scattering one's enemies. The day's older customs live in it too: the Shastra Puja, the worship of weapons and tools, and the Seemollanghana, the ritual crossing of the village boundary. Through it the devotee asks for Sri Rama's lasting grace and protection. Thursday is the traditional day, and Navratri its wider season.
dwadash-stotra-madhva: These twelve stotras are foundational to the Dvaita Vaishnava world. Composed by Shri Madhvacharya, the द्वादश स्तोत्र (मध्वाचार्य) is recited daily by Madhva Brahmins within their Sandhya Vandana, and the Haridasa tradition of Karnataka holds it as a supreme devotional text. Each stotra approaches Narayana from its own philosophical angle, so that across the set a coherent vision of Vishnu-tattva and Dvaita thought takes shape. Read here across 10 verses, about six minutes, the work is as much instruction as praise. Lakshmi is honoured alongside Vishnu. Suited to daily recitation and especially to Thursdays, it gains particular weight on Madhva Navami and Vaikuntha Ekadashi.
dwadasha-jyotirlinga-stotram: Twelve sacred lights, one short prayer. द्वादश ज्योतिर्लिंग स्तोत्रम्, from the Shiva Purana, names in turn the twelve Jyotirlingas — Somnath, Mahakaleshwar, Kashi Vishwanath, Rameshwaram, and the rest — so that reciting it becomes a pilgrimage made in words. The text is very brief, 4 verses and about 2 minutes, which keeps it well within reach of daily worship. It invokes Shiva in all his forms and asks for divine protection and auspiciousness. Tradition holds that the recitation wipes away sin and stores up spiritual merit, the kind a true pilgrimage would yield. Mondays are the natural day, and Maha Shivaratri the occasion when these twelve names carry the deepest charge.
ekadashi-mahatmya: Why does the eleventh lunar day matter so much to Vaishnavas? The एकादशी माहात्म्य, from the Uttara Khanda of the Padma Purana, answers fully. It tells the origin of Ekadashi from Vishnu's own divine power, lists all twenty-four Ekadashis of the year with their names and months, and explains the practices that surround the fast. Its claims are bold: a single Ekadashi fast is said to outweigh the merit of thousands of Ashvamedha Yagyas, and to lift even the gravest sins, brahmahatya among them, while the night vigil — Ratri Jagaran — multiplies that merit many times over. Read across 15 verses in about 15 minutes, with Lakshmi honoured beside Vishnu, it is the reference text devotees keep for every Ekadashi observance.
ganesh-bajrang: The name pairs Ganesha with the thunderbolt force of Bajrang, and the prayer lives up to it. श्री गणेश बजरंग, carried in lok parampara, strikes at obstacles and calamities with the weight of a vajra, clearing the way for the devotee against every adversity. Its 19 verses take about 10 minutes. Beyond protection, it draws wealth, prosperity, and auspiciousness into the household, fulfils the manoratha — the heartfelt wishes — of the one who recites it, and carries the joined blessings of riddhi and siddhi, shubh and labh. For anyone caught in a run of setbacks and failures, this is the Ganesha prayer to reach for. Wednesday is its day, and Ganesh Chaturthi its festival.
ganesh-bhujanga-stotram: Adi Shankaracharya cast this hymn in the swaying Bhujanga metre — the 'serpent' rhythm whose long, rolling lines give श्री गणेश भुजङ्ग स्तोत्रम् its musical pull. Across 7 verses, about 5 minutes, it draws a vivid poetic portrait of Ganesha's form while invoking Vighneshwara, the very lord of obstacles, with Parvati and Shiva nearby. The tradition recites it to clear obstacles and win success in one's undertakings, and to gain vidya, wealth, fame, and long life. Because of that, it is a natural choice to chant before beginning anything new. Tuesdays and Wednesdays suit it, and it has a settled place in Ganesh Chaturthi and Sankashti Chaturthi worship.
ganesh-chalisa: Forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, and with its framing dohas श्री गणेश चालीसा reaches its full 40 verses, about 15 minutes. The hymn praises Ganesha as the remover of obstacles and the giver of riddhi-siddhi — prosperity paired with spiritual attainment — while asking for wisdom, knowledge, and a clear, steady intellect. Devotees keep it for the peace and harmony it is said to bring into a household, for protection from negativity and evil, and for the honour and respect that, by tradition, follow daily recitation. Wednesday is its day, with Ganesh Chaturthi and Diwali the festivals when it is recited most. Read with attention, it asks Ganesha to smooth the path and steady the mind before any new endeavour.
